overall.summary: This chapter's quiz set is generally strong: grounded in the section text, appropriately intermediate for chapter 9/33, and usually focused on systems reasoning rather than rote recall. The remaining issues are mostly localized—some over-explaining of prior concepts, a few weak FILL/TF items, and a handful of learning objectives that are slightly tautological or vague.

missed_buildup_opportunities.explanation: The stem asks what bottleneck SSL removes 'compared with active learning and semi-supervised learning.' At chapter 9, both terms are already well-established in prior vocabulary or earlier in this same chapter; the comparison is valid, but the phrasing slightly re-teaches surrounding context rather than assuming reader familiarity.

The answer re-explains compression behavior at a fairly basic level ('pruning later removes redundant features') instead of leaning more directly on the prior-chapter concept and focusing on the new interaction introduced here.

As a summary question this is acceptable, but the answer spends space reintroducing model compression at a basic level rather than assuming knowledge from the prior chapter arc and emphasizing the chapter-to-chapter handoff.

build_up.chapter_level_assessment: Overall, the quizzes do leverage prior context appropriately, especially the iron law, D·A·M ordering, hardware bottlenecks, and distributed-training ideas. The chapter does not feel isolated, though a few answers still over-explain concepts that a reader at chapter 9 should already recognize.

distribution.type_mix_assessment: slightly over-MCQ and under-ORDER/FILL, but still reasonably close to target at chapter scale; SHORT coverage is healthy

per_question_issues.description: The FILL asks for 'amortized,' which is largely term recall from an explicit sentence pattern rather than conceptual inference. The surrounding context makes the answer nearly inevitable, so it under-tests systems reasoning.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Replace with a SHORT or MCQ that asks when amortization makes SSL economically favorable, e.g. compare scratch training versus pretrain-once/fine-tune-many across several downstream tasks.

per_question_issues.description: This FILL also targets 'amortized,' again testing named-term recall more than cost-model reasoning. It duplicates the same concept-and-term pairing already used in the self-supervised section.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Convert to a scenario-based MCQ or SHORT asking how ROI changes as the number of retraining runs increases, or why a one-time negative ROI can become positive over time.

per_question_issues.description: The blank 'compute-optimal ____' is answerable by rote phrase completion and tests the label of the diagnostic boundary more than its use. It is acceptable but weaker than the section's stronger scenario-based items.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Ask the learner to diagnose whether a run is data-starved or compute-starved from a described operating point, or to interpret what being below the frontier implies.

per_question_issues.description: The TF item is quite direct and close to the section's explicit rule of thumb: more gains from longer training imply compute-starved. It tests recall of a heuristic more than misconception-resistant reasoning.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Make it a more discriminating scenario, e.g. contrast two runs with different plateau behaviors and ask which intervention—better data or more compute—fits each.

per_question_issues.description: The statement is true-to-section and not wrong, but it is somewhat obvious once 'slightly different model checkpoints' are mentioned. It lacks the bite of a stronger misconception-focused TF item.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Sharpen it into a more plausible misconception, such as claiming that local uncertainty rankings remain globally valid as long as all workers share the same architecture.

per_question_issues.description: In the data echoing MCQ, 'Switch to more random sample access so storage latency averages out across workers' is not a very plausible distractor for an informed reader and feels weaker than the other options. That makes the item closer to a 3-option question.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Replace it with a more credible bottleneck-confusion distractor, such as increasing data-loader workers or precomputing augmentations to disk, which better competes with data echoing.

per_question_issues.description: The distractor claiming SSL removes 'any model architecture choices' is too obviously wrong and does not reflect a realistic practitioner misconception. The other distractors are stronger.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Swap in a more plausible error, such as claiming SSL mainly helps because unlabeled data is always distribution-matched and therefore requires less curation.

per_question_issues.description: The learning objective, 'Justify data selection's position at the head of the optimization stack,' is very close to simply restating the question stem. It names the same construct without adding a more concrete performance outcome.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Rewrite the LO around a testable outcome, e.g. 'Explain how upstream data reduction propagates into downstream training, compression, and deployment costs.'

per_question_issues.description: The LO 'Summarize the chapter's central systems argument for data selection' mirrors the stem almost exactly. It is valid but not especially specific or Bloom-rich beyond 'summarize.'
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Make the LO more concrete, such as 'Explain how upstream data curation compounds with downstream algorithm and hardware optimizations.'

per_question_issues.description: The LO 'Explain how data selection connects to the next stage of optimization in the D·A·M stack' is directionally fine but broad and somewhat abstract. It could specify the transition to model compression more concretely.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Revise to 'Compare data selection's workload-reduction role with model compression's algorithm-cost reduction role in the D·A·M stack.'
